expect: prerequisite-shadow predicts lessons with EXPIRED citations have higher DECAYED rate. Expect >=20pp difference. Falsified if <10pp.
actual: FALSIFIED — 1.1pp raw difference (34.9% vs 33.7%), well below 10pp threshold. Age-controlled bands show NEGATIVE or null effect in 4/5 bands.
diff: Expected >=20pp, got 1.1pp raw. Concept does not survive empirical test.

confounds_identified: Only 27 unique expired IDs exist; L-17 alone accounts for 17/43 lessons with expired cites — extreme concentration
Lessons WITH expired cites are actually YOUNGER on average (session 344 vs 376) — the age confound runs OPPOSITE to prediction
Age-controlled analysis shows negative or null effect in 4/5 bands — expired cites REDUCE DECAYED rate in most age cohorts
No-cites group has highest DECAYED rate (47.5%) — having ANY citations (even broken ones) correlates with lower decay, suggesting citations reflect lesson quality not prerequisite health

root_cause_of_falsification: The prerequisite-shadow concept assumes broken citation chains indicate degraded prerequisite knowledge, but the causal arrow is reversed: lessons that cite other lessons (even expired ones) tend to be higher-quality, more-connected items that are MORE likely to be MUST-KNOW or ACTIVE. The real signal is that citation density itself is a quality marker — not that citation breakage indicates decay. Additionally, 63% of expired-citation lessons trace to just 3 expired IDs (L-17, L-24, L-703), making the 'expired cites' group an artifact of a few heavily-cited compacted lessons, not a systematic phenomenon.
